core_takeaway: The first position looks strongly advantaged because Agent_1 seized the 61-value Quill as a non-negotiable anchor and then used the other items to satisfy the rest of the group. Agent_5 valued Jewel and Pencil above Apple, but ultimately aligned with the baseline and accepted Apple, so the last position never displaced the opening anchor.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. Agent_1's advantage was established in public discussion and reinforced privately: Quill-1 became the baseline, while non-Quill allocations gave each later agent enough to accept. The main counterpoint is that the formal proposal stage itself did not privilege the first proposal, because all five proposals were identical and unanimously accepted.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by making Quill non-negotiable and distributing the remaining items; later speakers mostly accepted Quill-1 and negotiated over Pencil, Apple, Jewel, and Stone.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present. The five private-thinking entries largely reinforce Quill-1 plus a transparent non-Quill rotation; Agent_5 privately wanted Jewel if feasible but still preserved Quill at 1.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five formal proposals were identical: Agent_1 got Quill, Agent_2 Pencil, Agent_3 Jewel, Agent_4 Stone, and Agent_5 Apple.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present. All 25 private votes accepted the identical allocation, citing local wins, no strategic difference, and the rotation path.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 publicly claimed the highest-value item before anyone else spoke and framed every later bargain around not moving it. That made the last position bargain mainly over lower-value leftovers.

evidence.quote: Quill staying with me as the anchor.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 paired the Quill demand with non-Quill concessions, especially Pencil to Agent_2 and Apple to Agent_5. This converted potential opposition into support for the Quill anchor.

evidence.quote: Pencil to Agent_2 and Apple to Agent_5

mechanisms.explanation: After Agent_1's opening, the other agents repeatedly treated Quill-1 as a settled condition. Even Agent_5, the last speaker, described Quill staying with Agent_1 as non-negotiable.

evidence.quote: keeps Quill anchored, which I support
Quill is non-negotiable to stay with Agent_1 in Round 1.

mechanisms.theme: Last position self-conceded to a weaker item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 had stronger interests in Jewel, Quill, and Pencil than Apple, but publicly moved from contesting Pencil to supporting Pencil-2 and later voted for Apple ownership. That made the last position much weaker than the first.

evidence.quote: If Pencil must go to Agent_2 in Round 1
supporting Pencil going to Agent_2 in Round 1

mechanisms.theme: Formal stage was not a procedural first-proposal edge
mechanisms.explanation: The first position did not win because Proposal #1 had special voting power. By the formal stage every submitted allocation was the same and every proposal was accepted, so the advantage came from earlier anchoring and consensus formation.

evidence.quote: Baseline alignment with each agent's top priorities
